notes.anchor: Had it been your own son's standing that was at issue
notes.note: Plancus refers to Cicero's son Marcus, studying in Athens at this time; the comparison flatters Cicero by equating his advocacy for Plancus with a father's partiality.

notes.anchor: sent my brother on ahead with three thousand horse
notes.note: Plancus's brother, known in the sources as L. Plotius Plancus (born Munatius, adopted into the Plotia gens), commanded the advance cavalry; he later became a notable rhetorician.

notes.anchor: Brutus and Mutina freed from siege
notes.note: Decimus Iunius Brutus Albinus, one of Caesar's assassins, had been blockaded in Mutina (Modena) by Antony since autumn 44 BC; the battles of Forum Gallorum and Mutina (14 and 21 April 43 BC) broke the siege.

The Allobroges were a powerful Gallic tribe controlling the territory between the Rhone and the Alps (modern Dauphiné); their territory gave Plancus a blocking position against any advance by Antony or Lepidus toward Italy via the Rhone corridor.
Caesar's celebrated Legio X, one of the most experienced units in the Roman army; its veterans were susceptible to Antonian appeals given their long personal loyalty to Caesar's line.

M. Iuventius Laterensis, a senior officer serving with or near Lepidus's command; Plancus used him as an intermediary to persuade Lepidus to remain loyal to the Senate. He killed himself when Lepidus defected to Antony in May 43 BC.
